bibliographic_data.title: Simulating the M-R Relation from APF follow up of TESS targets: Survey design and strategies for overcoming mass biases
bibliographic_data.abstract: We present simulations of multi-year RV follow up campaigns of the TESS small exoplanet yield on the Automated Planet Finder telescope, using four different schemes to sample the transiting planets’ RV phase curves. For planets below roughly 10 M⊕ we see a systematic bias of measured masses that are higher than the true planet mass, regardless of the observing scheme used. This produces a statistically significant difference in the mass-radius relation we recover, where planet masses are predicted to be too high and too similar across the entire super-Earth to Neptune radius range. This bias is due in part to only reporting masses that are measured with high statistical significance. Incorporating all mass measurements, even those that are essentially only upper limits, significantly mitigates this bias.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents simulations of RV follow-up campaigns for TESS targets to understand biases in mass measurements and strategies to mitigate these biases.
core_content.key_methodology: Four different observing schemes were used to sample the RV phase curves of transiting planets, with a focus on mitigating biases in mass measurements by incorporating all mass measurements, even those that are essentially only upper limits.
outcomes: A systematic bias was observed where measured masses for planets below roughly 10 M⊕ are higher than true planet masses.
Incorporating all mass measurements, including upper limits, significantly mitigates this bias.
Prioritization schemes that more evenly sample the RV phase curves produce a larger number of significant mass detections.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The paper discusses simulations of multi-year radial velocity (RV) follow-up campaigns for TESS targets using different observing schemes. The authors found a systematic bias in measured masses, where planets below 10 M⊕ have higher measured masses than their true values. This bias is mitigated by including all mass measurements, even those that are only upper limits. The study also highlights the importance of evenly sampling the RV phase curves to maximize significant mass detections.
